region_of_origin: India — Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'coffee beverage' class (FOODON_03301080); no specific South Indian filter coffee subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers prepared coffee beverages broadly; no specific South Indian filter coffee entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34731007 refers to coffee as a substance; used as the closest defensible parent anchor for this prepared beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of South Indian filter coffee is approximately 150 ml (about 150 g), served in a traditional dabarah-and-tumbler set.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values for South Indian filter coffee (milk-based, sweetened) adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' for a typical preparation of approximately 60% milk, 35% decoction, and 5% sugar by volume. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) used as secondary cross-reference for milk and coffee nutrient fractions including fatty acid profile and cholesterol.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for South Indian filter coffee. GI of 30 is estimated by analogy with milky sweetened coffee beverages reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). The low GI reflects the moderating effect of milk fat and protein on glucose absorption, and the relatively small amount of added sugar per serving. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (30 × 6.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content; caffeine may cause transient blood pressure elevation
hypertension.guidance: South Indian filter coffee contains only ~35 mg sodium per 100 g and provides potassium (150 mg) and calcium (85 mg) from milk, which are beneficial for blood pressure regulation; however, individuals with hypertension should limit intake to 1–2 cups per day due to caffeine's transient pressor effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar raises postprandial glucose; low GI mitigated by milk fat and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: The traditional preparation contains added sugar (~6.2 g sugar per 100 g), which can elevate postprandial blood glucose; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request unsweetened or sugar-substitute versions, and moderate consumption to one serving per day to avoid glycemic excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from milk; coffee polyphenols may confer cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 150 ml serving provides approximately 2.25 g saturated fat, which is modest within a heart-healthy diet; habitual moderate coffee consumption (1–2 cups/day) has been associated with reduced cardiovascular risk in epidemiological studies, but individuals with uncontrolled dyslipidaemia should monitor total dairy fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Coffee polyphenols with potential anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Coffee polyphenols may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefit relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; however, excessive caffeine intake may disrupt sleep quality, which is important for managing RA symptoms, so consumption should be limited to morning or early afternoon.

llm_training.natural_language_description: South Indian Filter Coffee, locally known as 'Kaapi', is a traditional hot beverage originating from the southern states of India — particularly Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, and Andhra Pradesh. It is prepared using a two-chambered stainless-steel drip filter: finely ground coffee powder, typically blended with 10–30% chicory, is packed into the upper chamber and boiling water is poured over it to slowly drip through, producing a thick, concentrated coffee decoction. This decoction is then combined with hot, frothy whole milk (roughly 60% milk to 35% decoction by volume) and sweetened with sugar. The beverage is traditionally served in a 'dabarah' (a wide, flat-bottomed metal cup) and tumbler set, and is poured back and forth from a height to aerate the liquid, creating a characteristic frothy top and cooling it to a comfortable drinking temperature. The flavour profile is bold, aromatic, and slightly bitter from the coffee and chicory, balanced by the sweetness of sugar and the creamy richness of boiled milk. Nutritionally, a 150 ml serving provides approximately 82 kcal, 3.3 g protein, 3.6 g fat, and 9.75 g carbohydrate, with meaningful calcium (127 mg) and potassium (225 mg) contributions from milk. The glycemic index is estimated at 30 (low), moderated by milk fat and protein. Clinically, the beverage is suitable for most adults in moderation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should opt for unsweetened versions, and those with hypertension should limit caffeine intake. Filter coffee is a culturally significant daily ritual in South Indian households and is commonly paired with breakfast items such as idli and dosa.
common_user_queries: How is South Indian filter coffee made?
How many calories in filter coffee?
Is filter coffee good for diabetes?
Is filter coffee high in sugar?
Filter coffee vs instant coffee nutrition
Can I drink filter coffee with hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' for a typical South Indian filter coffee preparation. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact beverage as a composite dish; values are derived from the known ingredient proportions (milk, decoction, sugar) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for milky sweetened coffee. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a typical South Indian filter coffee preparation: approximately 60% whole milk, 35% coffee decoction (coffee-chicory blend), and 5% sugar by volume, yielding ~150 g per serving. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/trace for this liquid beverage preparation per ICMR-NIN reference data. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible in this preparation per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy whole milk data. 4. water_g set to -1: detailed moisture data for this composite beverage preparation is not separately reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024; the beverage is predominantly water by mass but a precise per-100g figure for this specific composite preparation is not available in any approved source. 5. saturated_fat_g (1.5), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.6), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy whole milk fatty acid profile scaled to the milk fraction in the beverage. 6. cholesterol_mg (8.0) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy whole milk data scaled to the milk fraction. 7. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for South Indian filter coffee was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 8. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been removed as this beverage contains ~6.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g (9.75 g per 150 ml serving), which is inconsistent with ketogenic dietary thresholds. 9. Duplicate portion entries in the input have been deduplicated to a single 150 ml serving. 10. This is an Indian dish; multilingual naming rules for South Indian cuisine apply: Tamil and Hindi local names are provided. Kannada script alias (ಫಿಲ್ಟರ್ ಕಾಫಿ) is included in aliases as the beverage is strongly associated with Karnataka. 11. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT as no exact subclass for South Indian filter coffee exists in any of these ontologies.
